questions.prompt: Complete: "Scarcely ___ the ship left port when the storm struck."
questions.explanation: Scarcely triggers inversion with the past perfect: Scarcely had the ship left port when… The main event (the storm) is also past, so the past perfect auxiliary had is correct. "Did" would need a base verb (leave, not left). "Has" is present perfect — wrong for a completed past narrative. "Was" does not collocate with the following verb left in this structure.

questions.prompt: Complete: "So ___ the conditions that the race was called off."
questions.explanation: The "So + adjective + inversion" pattern requires: So + adjective (terrible) + inverted verb (were) + subject (the conditions) + that… So the correct answer is "terrible were". "Were terrible" places the verb before the adjective — wrong order. "Terrible they were" inserts the subject pronoun between adjective and verb, which is not used in this inverted structure. "Were the terrible" is not grammatical.

options.text: Only when did the fire brigade arrive the building was saved.
Only when the fire brigade arrived, the building was saved.
Only when the fire brigade arrived was the building saved.
Only when was the fire brigade arriving the building saved.

questions.explanation: With "Only when", inversion applies to the MAIN clause, not the when-clause: Only when [the fire brigade arrived] was the building saved (auxiliary before subject). Option (a) wrongly inverts inside the when-clause ("did… arrive"). Option (b) has normal order in the main clause — no inversion, which is wrong after the fronted only when. Option (d) inverts both clauses, which is never correct.

questions.prompt: Complete: "Not only ___ the experiment, but she also published her findings within a month."
questions.explanation: After "Not only" the first clause must invert: auxiliary (did) + subject (she) + base verb (complete). This gives: Not only did she complete the experiment… Option (a) keeps normal subject-first order — no inversion. Option (b) also keeps subject before auxiliary — still no inversion. Option (c) inverts without the do-auxiliary — this is not grammatical in modern English for action verbs.

questions.prompt: Complete: "Such ___ his influence that the whole industry changed direction."
questions.explanation: "Such was…" is the inverted past form: Such was his influence that… The verb must be in the simple past to match the past narrative (the industry changed direction — also past simple). "Is" makes it present, breaking the narrative tense. "Had been" signals past-before-past (past perfect), implying a prior event not expressed here. "Would be" introduces a conditional that has no supporting if-clause.

questions.prompt: A safety notice reads: "___ tampering with the emergency exits. Doing so may endanger lives." Which opening is most appropriate AND correctly formed?

options.text: Not only should you refrain from
On no account should passengers engage in
Never you should be involved in

questions.explanation: "On no account should passengers engage in tampering with the emergency exits" is correctly formed ("On no account" + modal inversion: should + subject) and idiomatically appropriate for a formal safety notice expressing absolute prohibition. Option (a) produces "Not only should you refrain from tampering…" — grammatically inverted but the "Not only" structure requires a second clause ("but also…") which is absent. Option (c) puts the subject before the modal ("Never you should") — no inversion. Option (d) "Rarely" softens the prohibition to a habit observation — wrong for a safety notice demanding total compliance.
